topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. I reread the subject line three times, my thumb hovering over the screen, as if the words themselves might vaporize. 'Campus Closure Notice.' The words swam before my eyes, blurring the familiar logo of the university I’d poured four years of my life into. I sat back in my desk chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my sudden stillness. My hands, which had been gripping my lukewarm coffee mug with practiced ease, began to tremble uncontrollably. I slowly pushed the laptop away, the sound of the click echoing unnervingly loud in the quiet dorm room. I stood up, pacing the small space between my bed and the window, my breath coming in shallow, quick gasps. The reality wasn't settling in; it was actively fracturing the air around me.

[story 2]

"Wait, what?" I managed to croak out, the sound barely audible above the distant hum of the campus library. Professor Davies’ announcement had been delivered with a practiced, almost cheerful tone, but when the details about the impending shutdown were laid out, the color drained from my face. It wasn't just disappointing; it was a physical disorientation. I blinked rapidly, trying to force the information to cohere into something sensible. My mouth went dry. I looked down at my hands, noticing the faint tremor that had started in my fingertips and was now running up my forearms. I walked to the window, staring out at the quad where students usually milled about, now eerily empty. A strange, cold numbness spread through my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath.

[story 3]

I kept staring at the printed notice taped to my refrigerator, tracing the bold letters with my index finger. Every line seemed to distort, the familiar architecture of my educational journey collapsing into meaningless shapes. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscles ache. I started to pace the small apartment, a restless energy churning beneath my skin, an urgent need to run, but my feet felt rooted to the linoleum floor. I stopped abruptly in the middle of the room, my shoulders slumping forward as a heavy weight settled upon them. A low, involuntary sound escaped my throat—a sound somewhere between a sigh and a choked sob. The world outside the window seemed too bright, too loud, and utterly unreal.
